1: Joash begins to reign at 7 years old and reigns 40 years in Jerusalem — the number 40 carrying wilderness-wandering and covenant-testing resonance. The child-king installed by the faithful priest has the longest reign of Judah's genuinely restored kings until Uzziah's 52 years. But the 40-year reign is divided: the first half under Jehoiada's guidance is faithful; the second half after Jehoiada's death apostatizes. The divided reign of Joash is the OT's clearest portrait of conditional, dependent faithfulness — a king who does right only so long as he has a faithful mediator. Christ's reign has no such dependency: his faithfulness is intrinsic, not borrowed from a mentor.
2: 'Joash did what was right in the eyes of YHWH all the days of Jehoiada the priest.' The qualification 'all the days of Jehoiada' is the Chronicler's most explicit statement of the problem of mediated faithfulness: the king's spiritual condition depends entirely on the continued presence of a godly intermediary. When the mediator dies, the king defaults to corruption. The new covenant addresses this structural problem: Christ the mediator does not die and leave the covenant community without mediation (Hebrews 7:24-25: 'he holds his priesthood permanently... he always lives to make intercession'). The permanent living mediation of Christ means the covenant community's faithfulness is no longer dependent on any mortal intermediary.
3: Jehoiada takes two wives for Joash, and he has sons and daughters — the perpetuation of the Davidic line through priestly arrangement. The priest securing the king's succession is an inversion of the usual order (kings secure priestly succession); here the priestly-royal partnership reverses the normal hierarchy so that the covenant line is preserved through priestly initiative. Melchizedek (Psalms 110:4; Heb 7) is the OT's archetype of the priest who is also king; in the Joash narrative, the priest secures the king's genealogical continuation. Christ as the Melchizedekian priest-king unifies the roles that Jehoiada and Joash divide between them.
4: Joash's heart was to restore the house of YHWH — the king's primary covenant ambition is the renewal of the sanctuary. The temple as the center of Davidic theological identity means its restoration is the most important royal act. The NT's temple theology transforms this: Christ's body is the temple (John 2:19-21), and the church as the body of Christ is the new temple (1 Corinthians 3:16-17; Ephesians 2:19-22). The restoration of the physical temple under Joash typifies the building of the eschatological temple through Christ's death and resurrection — the construction of a dwelling of God 'not made with hands' (Mark 14:58).
5: Joash sends the Levites through all Israel to collect money from all Israel for the annual repair of the temple — a tax that had apparently lapsed. The national collection for the temple's maintenance is an all-Israel affair: even the divided north is included in the royal letter (kol-yiśrāʾēl). The appeal to all Israel for the sanctuary's restoration types the universal scope of the new covenant's assembly: Christ gathers from all nations (Revelation 7:9), not merely Judah or even Israel, for the building of the eschatological sanctuary.
6: Joash summons Jehoiada the chief priest and charges him: 'Why have you not required the Levites to bring in from Judah and Jerusalem the tax levied by Moses the servant of God and the congregation of Israel for the tent of testimony?' The appeal to the Mosaic tent of testimony (ʾōhel hāʿēḏût) as the founding covenant structure that the temple continues invokes the wilderness sanctuary. Christ is the one in whom the original ʾōhel-presence dwells permanently — 'the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us' (John 1:14, using the Greek eskēnōsen = pitched his tent).
7: The sons of Athaliah, the wicked woman, had broken into the house of God and had also used all the dedicated things of the house of YHWH for the Baals. The temple vessels used in Baal worship during the interregnum are the OT's image of the sacred corrupted for profane use — the covenant instruments diverted to idolatrous purpose. Christ's cleansing of the temple (Matthew 21:12-13; John 2:14-16) addresses the same category of corruption: the Father's house made into 'a den of robbers,' the sacred space and its instruments diverted from their covenant purpose.
8: A chest is made at the king's command and set outside the gate of the house of YHWH — the collection vessel for the temple restoration fund. The chest placed at the threshold of the sanctuary is the point where the covenant community's resources are received and redirected toward the holy. The NT parallel is the widow's offering observed by Jesus (Mark 12:41-44): the treasury at the temple gate where contributions are deposited, and the one who gives everything she has to the restoration of the place where God is worshipped. Christ commends the widow's giving as the truest form of what Joash's chest represents.
9: The proclamation is made throughout Judah and Jerusalem to bring to YHWH the contribution that Moses the servant of God had imposed on Israel in the wilderness. The connection to the wilderness Mosaic collection — the half-shekel tax for the tabernacle (Ex 2 Chronicles 30:11-16) — roots Joash's temple fundraising in the founding Mosaic pattern. The half-shekel was revived in Second Temple Judaism (cf. Matthew 17:24-27: Peter's question about the temple tax, and Jesus' provision of the coin for both himself and Peter). Christ both fulfills and transcends the temple tax: he provides for it freely, while being 'the Son' who is technically exempt (Matthew 17:25-26).
10: All the officials and all the people rejoiced and brought their contributions, casting them into the chest until they had finished. The joyful freewill character of the giving — 'all... rejoiced' (wayyiśmᵉḥû kol-haśśārîm wᵉḵol-hāʿām) — distinguishes it from the reluctant tax-payment the Levites had failed to collect (2 Chronicles 24:5-6). The NT equivalent is Paul's theology of cheerful giving: 'Each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver' (2 Corinthians 9:7). The joyful temple collection under Joash is the OT pattern Paul applies to the new covenant community's support of the work of Christ.
11: Day after day the chest is brought to the king's officers, counted, emptied, and returned — a regular cycle of collection and disbursement for the temple work. The daily rhythm of the collection — recurring, consistent, institutional — types the new covenant's regular, sustained support of the ongoing work of the people of God. The daily collection that the early Jerusalem church maintained (Acts 6:1-2) follows the same institutional logic: a structured, regular distribution to support those who need and to fund the work of the community.
12: The king and Jehoiada give the collected money to the supervisors of the work for hiring stonecutters, carpenters, ironworkers, and bronze workers to restore the house of YHWH. The craftsman-teams assembled for temple restoration echo the original construction under Solomon: the same range of skilled trades, the same collaborative process of building the place of divine presence. Paul's image of the church as a building under construction ('each one's work will become manifest... it will be revealed by fire,' 1 Corinthians 3:12-15) draws on this OT temple-construction imagery for the new covenant community's ongoing building work.
13: The workers labor and the repair goes forward; they restore the house of God to its proper condition and strengthen it. The language 'its proper condition' (ʾel-mattᵉkuntô* — its proper measurement/specification) invokes the original building specifications as the standard to which the restored temple must conform. The restoration is not innovation but recovery of the founding intention. Christ comes not to abolish but to fulfill (Matthew 5:17) — the new covenant's restoration of the covenant relationship to its founding intention, undoing the distortion of sin rather than inventing new terms.
14: When they had finished, they brought the remaining silver to the king and Jehoiada; temple vessels are made from it — spoons, ladles, gold and silver vessels. The surplus from the restoration project produces new vessels for ongoing worship. The new covenant's surplus-creating logic: Christ's work not only restores what was lost but generates abundance that overflows into new expressions of worship (John 10:10: 'I came that they may have life and have it abundantly'). The restoration surplus creating new vessels types the abundance of the new creation that overflows from Christ's completed work.
15: Jehoiada grows old to the fullness of days: 130 years old, full of days. The extraordinary lifespan of Jehoiada — exceeding the patriarchal spans of the post-flood era and approaching the antediluvian ages — marks him as a figure of covenant completeness. One hundred and thirty years old at death is exactly the age at which Adam fathered Seth (Genesis 5:3), the genealogical ancestor through whom the covenant line runs to Noah. Jehoiada's life, dedicated entirely to preserving and restoring the Davidic covenant line, is the OT's most complete portrait of faithful priestly service — the priest who gives his life in service of the king types the interceding priest whose life sustains the covenant community.
16: Jehoiada is buried in the city of David among the kings because of the good he did in Israel, for God and his house. A priest buried among the kings — the single instance in Chronicles of a non-Davidic person receiving royal burial — is a Melchizedekian sign: the one who holds the priestly office has been granted the status of king. Psalms 110:4, cited in Heb 5-7, establishes that the promised Davidic king will be 'a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek' — combining the priestly and royal offices in one person. Jehoiada's burial in the royal tombs is the historical instance of the priest-king fusion that Ps 110 predicts and Christ fulfills.
17: After the death of Jehoiada, the princes of Judah came and paid homage to the king; then the king listened to them. The king's immediate susceptibility to elite pressure once his priestly mentor is gone reveals the structural fragility of Joash's character: his faithfulness was borrowed, not intrinsic. The princes who lead him astray are the same class that had been restrained by Jehoiada's authority. The NT pattern of communities that remain faithful 'as long as...' (cf. Judges 2:7: 'the people served YHWH all the days of Joshua') is the chronic problem of leader-dependent faith that the new covenant addresses through the internal dwelling of the Spirit (Jeremiah 31:33-34; Ezekiel 36:26-27).
18: They abandoned the house of YHWH, the God of their fathers, and served the Asherim and the idols; and wrath came upon Judah and Jerusalem because of their guilt. The abandonment of the restored temple — rebuilt at great effort in 2 Chronicles 24:4-14 — for Asherah worship in the same reign shows the pattern of reformation without transformation: external renewal without internal change cannot produce sustained covenant faithfulness. The new covenant addresses this through regeneration: 'I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit within you' (Ezekiel 36:26). The restoration of the temple building without the restoration of the heart repeats Joash's tragedy in each generation that does not receive the new covenant's gift.
19: YHWH sent prophets among them to bring them back to himself; they testified against them, but they would not pay attention. The repeated sending of prophets to the repeatedly unfaithful people — prophet after prophet, testimony after testimony — is the OT's most sustained expression of divine patience. Matthew 21:33-44 (the parable of the tenants) draws this pattern to its conclusion: the owner sends servant after servant, and finally sends his son. The progressive sending of prophets climaxes in the sending of the Son; the pattern of rejection reaches its worst moment when the Son is killed. The rejected prophets of Joash's reign are the OT chapter of the story that Hebrews 1:1-2 summarizes.
20: The Spirit of God clothed (lāḇᵉšāh) Zechariah the son of Jehoiada the priest, and he stood above the people and said to them: 'Thus says God...' The Spirit-clothing of Zechariah is the prophetic commissioning formula at its most dramatic — the Spirit as garment worn by the prophet who speaks in the divine first person. Isaiah 61:1 — 'The Spirit of the Lord YHWH is upon me, because YHWH has anointed me to bring good news' — is the same Spirit-clothing experienced by the Servant, cited by Christ in Luke 4:18-19 as the manifesto of his mission. The Spirit who clothes Zechariah to speak YHWH's word is the Spirit who anoints Christ to proclaim the Year of Jubilee — the same commissioning, the greater messenger.
21: But they conspired against him, and by command of the king they stoned him with stones in the court of the house of YHWH. The death of the Spirit-clothed prophet, the son of the faithful priest, killed by the king he served, in the temple court — is the OT's most concentrated martyrdom narrative. Jesus cites this event in Matthew 23:35: 'so that on you may come all the righteous blood shed on earth, from the blood of righteous Abel to the blood of Zechariah the son of Barachiah [= Jehoiada], whom you murdered between the sanctuary and the altar.' Zechariah's death marks the end of the OT martyrological sequence; the stoning in the temple court is the type of the ultimate prophet's death outside the city gate. The temple court becomes the place of anti-witness: the Spirit-speaker silenced in the Spirit's own sanctuary.
22: Joash did not remember the kindness (ḥesed) that Jehoiada his father had shown him but killed his son. Zechariah's dying words: 'May YHWH see and avenge!' (yireʾ YHWH wᵉyidrōš). The dying martyr's prayer for divine retribution stands at the end of the OT's martyrological tradition. Stephen's dying prayer (Acts 7:60: 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them') is the NT's answer — the same structural moment (martyr dying among accusers) but the prayer inverted from retribution to forgiveness, because Christ's own death has already absorbed the retribution the martyrs' blood required. Christ's 'Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do' (Luke 23:34) is the first word from the cross that enables Stephen's prayer and completes the martyrological sequence that Zechariah's death initiated.
23: At the end of the year the army of Aram comes up against Joash — a small force that defeats a great army of Judah, because they had forsaken YHWH the God of their fathers. The covenantal reversal — few defeating many, the weak overcoming the strong — enacts the holy-war logic in reverse: when the covenant is broken, the normal divine arithmetic of holy war (one chasing a thousand) inverts. Leviticus 26:8 promised that five of Israel would chase a hundred; Leviticus 26:36-37 promised that in judgment they would flee when none pursues. The Aramean victory with a small force over Judah's army is the covenant curse theology enacted, the divine holy-war guarantee withdrawn.
24: The Arameans came with few men, yet YHWH delivered a very great army into their hand, because they had forsaken YHWH the God of their fathers. The explicit divine causation — 'YHWH delivered' (nātan YHWH*) the great army into the few Arameans' hands — reaffirms divine agency even in covenant judgment. YHWH who gives victory to the faithful gives defeat to the faithless through the same sovereignty. The cross enacts this logic definitively: God 'did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all' (Romans 8:32), using the same vocabulary of divine giving (paredōken) to describe the supreme act of both judgment and grace.
25: The Arameans leave Joash severely wounded; his servants conspire against him for the blood of the son of Jehoiada the priest, and kill him in his bed. The blood of Zechariah — the martyr's prayer of 2 Chronicles 24:22 — is avenged through the king's own servants, who are motivated by the unjust killing. The king who rejected the covenant and killed the faithful prophet is destroyed by covenant-motivated vengeance. The NT parallel: those who reject and kill the prophets will face the blood-reckoning (Matthew 23:35-36) that reaches its climax in the judgment of Jerusalem in 70 CE. Covenant faithfulness vindicates the martyrs; covenant rejection accumulates blood-guilt that eventually falls on the perpetrators.
26: The conspirators who kill Joash are Zabad son of Shimeath the Ammonite and Jehozabad son of Shimrith the Moabite — their mothers are explicitly identified as foreign women. The Chronicler's inclusion of the foreign maternal lineage (Ammonite and Moabite) of the king's assassins recalls Ruth the Moabite and the complex inclusion of gentile lineage in the covenant community. Here the gentile-descended servants perform the function of covenant avengers, executing judgment on the king who violated the covenant. The nations serve as instruments of divine justice even when Israel fails — the pattern the prophets develop and Paul articulates: 'Has God rejected his people? By no means!' (Romans 11:1), but the nations have been ingrafted into the covenant's judicial function.
27: Joash's sons, the many oracles against him, and the rebuilding of the house of God are written in the commentary (midrāš) of the Book of the Kings. His son Amaziah reigns in his place. The reference to the midrāš (the only pre-rabbinic use of this term in the OT as a literary designation) for Joash's reign — specifically the temple restoration narrative — suggests that the temple rebuilding under Joash generated an independent homiletical-exegetical commentary tradition even in the pre-exilic period. The temple rebuilding is important enough to produce its own interpretive literature; the new temple built through Christ's death ('Destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it,' John 2:19-21) generates the NT's own midrashic tradition of interpretation — the epistles and gospels as midrāš on the founding event of the new covenant.
